topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on my kitchen counter, stark white paper wrapping a collection of aggressively cheerful sunflowers. No note. Just the vibrant, slightly wilting blooms demanding attention. I picked up the box, turning it over in my hands, the paper feeling flimsy, almost insulting. I glanced around the apartment—the usual clutter, the unwashed dishes—and felt a distinct tightening in my chest. Who sends unsolicited bouquets? It felt like a performance, a gesture requiring an explanation I wasn't prepared to give. I set the box down with a soft thud, my jaw setting tight as I considered whether to toss them in the recycling bin immediately.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed me the arrangement, his smile was too wide, almost theatrical. The roses were deep crimson, heavy with dew, and the sheer volume of them felt excessive. I took the package, my fingers brushing against the cool, velvety petals. I looked up at the courier, my expression carefully neutral, trying to project an air of detached professionalism. "Just flowers?" I managed, my voice flat. He just shrugged, a gesture that seemed to acknowledge the unspoken weight of the gift. I tucked the box into a spare corner of the hallway, my eyes already scanning the building for any sign of who might be orchestrating this elaborate display.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked into the entryway, placed precisely on the worn welcome mat. They were lilies, unnervingly perfect, their scent thick and overpowering in the otherwise stale air of my hallway. I approached slowly, my steps deliberate, cataloging the arrangement—the precise arrangement, the lack of any accompanying inscription. A low, involuntary sigh escaped my lips. I leaned in, inhaling deeply, trying to find some hidden meaning in the glossy green and creamy white. Nothing. It was just a lot of petals. I straightened up abruptly, running a hand over my forehead, feeling a distinct sense of being cornered by something utterly illogical.
